---
title: "project01"
name(s):  Jacqueline Caldwell, Micheal Bambha
class: BINF 6250; Fall 2025
output: 
  html_document: default
  pdf_document: default
  github_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 2
    html_preview: false
 ---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
library("reticulate")
use_python("/usr/bin/python3")
```

```{python Load Libraries}
from typing import List
from collections import Counter
from pprint import pprint
```

# Project 01: VCF File Parsing

The purpose of this file is to find rare alleles with the key `AF_EXAC`
in a ClinVar VCF file, identify diseases associated with these rare alleles,
and then tally the total number of diseases found throughout the VCF.

We have implemented three functions (one helper) to accomplish this. We read
the file line by line, parse the last column of the file into a dictionary,
find the `AF_EXAC` key, and return the list of diseases. Then, using
`Counter` from the `collections` library, we can update the `Counter` object
every line to count the diseases.

## parse_line

The last column of a VCF file is structured as key-value pairs denoted with
"=", and the pairs are separated by ";". As VCF files are tab-delimited,
we can grab the last column with `.split("\t")[7]`. You could also index it
with `[-1]` but since VCF format is always supposed to have 8 columns, 
I chose to be explicit in grabbing with `[7]` as we can also use this as a
check in case one of the lines is formatted wrong for whatever reason.

Next, we grab the key-value pairs by splitting on `";"`, and we can add
some functionality with `filter(None)` which will get rid of any empty
strings. Now that we have a list of strings in format `"key=value"`, using
`split("=", 1)` will split this into another list. The `1` positional argument
refers to the number of times we split. Since we are iterating by item
in the list, we expect there to be only one `=`, so this is just a way to ensure
that unexpected `=` won't break our function.

Lastly, we will get the value using `.get()` as it will return None if
the key doesn't exist. Since we don't expect `AF_EXAC` to be in the line
every time, this is a much better solution than doing `attrs[key] = value` since
that will raise an error if the key doesn't exist.

Since the value of `AF_EXAC` is a string, we have to cast it to `float` before
evaluating the allele frequency. 

```{python parse_line}
def parse_line(vcf_string: str) -> List[str]:
    """Parse a line of a VCF file for the allele
    frequency AF_EXAC and return a list of diseases
    for that entry if the allele frequency is
    < 0.0001.

    Args:
        vcf_string (str): A non-metadata line of a VCF
        file (tab-delimited). Example:

        1	1014451	475281	C	T	.	.	AF_EXAC=0.0077226;CLNDN=Immunodeficiency_38_with_basal_ganglia_calcification

    Returns:
        List[str]: List of diseases found or None if nothing was found.
    """
    attrs = {}

    try:
        info = vcf_string.split("\t")[7]
    except IndexError:  # in case the line is incorrectly formatted, we return an empty list
        return []

    for part in filter(
        None, info.strip().split(";")
    ):  # if .strip().split() returns "", filter will remove from list
      # filter(None, ...) evaluates truthiness, not just explicit None
        if "=" in part:
            k, v = part.split("=", 1)
            attrs[k.strip()] = v.strip()

    allele_freq = attrs.get("AF_EXAC")

    if allele_freq is None:
        return []

    if float(allele_freq) < 0.0001:
        return _filter_CLNDN(attrs["CLNDN"])

    return []
```

## _filter_CLNDN helper function

I added an additional helper function to filter out the CLNDN key
if the disease was `not_specified` or `not_provided`. Diseases are separated
by `"|"` so we just use `.split("|")` to return a list of all of the diseases. 

```{python _filter_CLNDN helper}
def _filter_CLNDN(clndn_str: str) -> List[str]:
    """Take in the value of CLNDN parsed out of a VCF file
    and return a list of diseases.

    Args:
        clndn_str (str): The value of the CLNDN parsed from
        key/value hierarchy of VCF formatted files. Contains
        a string of diseases separated by "|".
        Example: Myasthenic_syndrome|Breast_cancer

    Returns:
        List[str]: List of diseases parsed by the "|".
        Diseases that are "not_specified" or "not_provided" are
        not returned.
    """
    strings_to_filter = ["not_specified", "not_provided"]
    clndn_str_split = clndn_str.split("|")
    return [
        disease for disease in clndn_str_split if disease not in strings_to_filter
    ]
```

## read_file

Lastly, to bring everything together, a simple solution to count the diseases
is the `Counter` object. Calling `Counter` on a list gives us essentially
a dictionary of the counts with added functionality. Calling `.update()` on
each new list will update the counts. 

```{python read_file}
def read_file(file_name: str) -> Counter[str]:
    """Read in a VCF file and return a dictionary of
    diseases counts found to be associated with allele
    frequencies of < 0.0001. Counts represent the number
    that each associated disease was found across
    all variants.

    Args:
        file_name (str): VCF file to read

    Returns:
        Counter[str]: Dictionary of disease counts
        from all variants. Example:
        {"Breast_cancer": 20, ...}
    """

    disease_count = Counter()
    with open(file_name, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        for line in f:
            if line.startswith("#"):
                continue
            diseases = parse_line(line)
            if diseases:
                disease_count.update(diseases)

    return disease_count
```

```{python Run Main}
if __name__ == "__main__":
    pprint(read_file("clinvar_20190923_short.vcf"))
```
